text: Red dye number three gives its vibrant color to a variety of products, many of which appeal to children like candy, sprinkles, cakes with fun colors, fruit inspired treats.
It's also found in medications.
Red three is derived from petroleum.
The U .S.
Food and Drug Administration, or FDA, approved its use in food in 1969.
70 % of our grocery stores are ultra -processed foods.
Most of them, if not all of them, contain one of the nine synthetic food dyes.
Last month, the FDA banned the coloring in all food and drugs.
Studies show that the substance causes cancer in male lab rats.
These things can actually cause damage.
These things are synthetic, meaning they're xenobiotics, meaning they're foreign to the the body,
meaning they'll stimulate inflammation, and inflammation is intimately tied to carcinogenesis.
Which is the start of cancer formation.
Europe banned Red 3 from foods in 1994.
The additive was banned in U .S.
cosmetics in 1990.
The food ban in America was delayed in part by industry pressure, according to Hofseth.
A lot of lobbyists pushing back against regulations against synthetic food dies, which is a big, big industry.
The FDA ban mandates that manufacturers reformulate food without Red 3, starting in January 2027, and ingested drugs a year later.
We have the opportunity to replace it with things that likely have no harmful effects, and that's the hope.
A great reformulation, I hope, is a great reformulation because it reimagines products from the ground up.
That means getting used to new ways to inject color into food, like using natural food coloring, or getting used to less colorful aisles in the grocery store.
Certain things might look a little different, and they might look actually how they're probably supposed to look, might taste in cases a little bit different.
In some cases, Red 3 might be replaced by Red 40, another synthetic food dye.
In Europe, foods containing REDD -40 currently require a warning label that the product may cause hyperactivity in children.
Dora McQuarr, VOA News, Washington.
